title: SEGMENT 6 — Red fluorescent proteins from coral: DsRed and its difficulties

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Red fluorescent proteins from coral: DsRed and its difficulties
In 1999, a Russian group led by Sergey Lukyanov went looking, systematically, at fluorescent and colored proteins in other marine organisms beyond the jellyfish.
And they found that certain reef-building corals — animals in the genus Discosoma — make fluorescent proteins that emit much further into the red than anything Aequorea produces.
The most famous of these was DsRed, "Discosoma red," which emits at about 583 nanometers, in the deep orange-red.
Suddenly, the spectrum had a true red entry.
Mechanistically, DsRed turned out to be a cousin of GFP.
Same beta-barrel fold — the same protein soda can.
Same general idea of an internal chromophore formed from a similar amino acid triad.
But the maturation chemistry goes one step further than GFP.

After a GFP-like green chromophore forms, an additional oxidation step happens — another double bond is added — and that extra bond extends the conjugated electronic system further, which pulls the absorption and emission deeper into the red.
So DsRed is essentially "GFP plus one more cooking step.
" It tells you something elegant about evolution — the basic design has been independently selected in jellyfish and in coral, with small chemical variations giving different colors.
But DsRed had problems.
Serious ones.
The problems are worth listing, because they set up the next big chapter of engineering work.
Problem one: DsRed was a tetramer.
Tetramer means four copies of the protein bind tightly to each other and act as a single complex.
Picture four soda cans stuck together.
That's catastrophic for fusion-protein work.
The whole point of a fluorescent tag is to attach it to your favorite protein and watch where your favorite protein goes.
But if the tag forms tetramers, then every time you tag your protein, you're actually dragging it into a four-way assembly, because the tag is yanking itself together with three other tagged copies.
You are no longer studying your protein.
You are studying your protein artificially clamped into a four-protein cluster.
The tag has changed the biology.
This is the moment to introduce the broader principle, because it'll come up over and over.
A good fluorescent tag is supposed to be invisible to the biology.
It's supposed to be a passive observer that goes everywhere your protein goes, without affecting anything.
The moment the tag starts pulling your protein into artificial groupings, the tag is lying.
It's telling you a story that is more about the tag than about your protein.
Problem two: DsRed matured extremely slowly.
Glow chemistry took more than a day in newly-synthesized molecules.
And along the way, the protein passed through a green-fluorescent intermediate state before finally turning red.
So if you imaged early, you got green.
If you imaged late, you got red.
If you imaged in the middle, you got a confusing mixture.
For watching a process unfold over hours, this was awful.
Problem three: DsRed was prone to aggregation and had finicky photochemistry.
So you had a true red fluorescent protein, finally — but it was a tetramer that took a day to glow and tended to clump.
You could not really use it for serious fusion-protein experiments.
The big question for the field became: could you engineer DsRed into a well-behaved monomer that matured fast, the way EGFP had been engineered out of wild-type GFP?
The answer turned out to be yes — but it required heroic protein engineering.
The Tsien lab spent years on it.
They had to introduce dozens of mutations — over thirty in some versions — to break up all the tetramer-forming surfaces without destroying the chromophore or the fold.
Every time they disrupted one interface, they had to make sure the protein still folded properly, still matured, and still glowed red.
It was a relentless slog of mutation, screening, mutation, screening.
The first true monomer they produced was called mRFP1 — monomeric red fluorescent protein 1 — and it came out in 2002.
It was, finally, a single soda can, not a four-can cluster.
But it was dim.
It matured incompletely.
Useful, but not great.
mRFP1 was the foundation for what came next, and what came next is one of the great stories of systematic protein engineering.

The Tsien lab took mRFP1 as a starting point and pushed it through round after round of random mutagenesis and screening, producing a family of monomeric red and orange variants that they named, with characteristic humor, after fruits.
The mFruits.
That's the next segment.
But before we leave coral and move on, I want you to take a structural lesson with you.

The fact that a coral and a jellyfish — animals separated by hundreds of millions of years of evolution — both encode fluorescent proteins built on the same beta-barrel scaffold, with chromophores formed by similar autocatalytic chemistry from internal amino acid triads, tells you something.
It tells you that the barrel-with-internal-chromophore is a robust evolved solution to the problem of making a protein-based glow stick.
It has been independently arrived at in multiple marine lineages.
We now know fluorescent proteins from many corals, from anemones, from copepods, even from a single-celled organism.
There is enormous natural diversity, and the Tsien lab realized that this natural diversity was the raw material from which any color could be engineered.
That's the move that gave us the modern rainbow.
Let's see how it turned out.
